serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Nan Gyi Thoke weighs approximately 350 g and contains thick round rice noodles tossed with chicken curry sauce, chickpea flour, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: thick rice noodles (FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table), chicken curry (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy), chickpea flour, fish sauce, and garnishes; proportions based on standard Burmese recipe documentation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Nan Gyi Thoke exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA values for rice noodles and USDA SR Legacy values for chicken curry, chickpea flour, fish sauce, onion, egg, and garnishes. Approximate composition: 45% thick rice noodles (cooked), 30% chicken curry sauce, 10% chickpea flour paste, 5% egg, 5% fried onion, 5% condiments and herbs.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice noodles and mixed noodle dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles (GI ~53) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nan Gyi Thoke. GI estimated at 53 based on the closest analogous food: cooked rice noodles (GI 53, University of Sydney GI Database). The dish is predominantly thick rice noodles (~45% by weight); the addition of protein (chicken, egg) and fat (curry oil) is expected to moderate glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.3, rounded to 34.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and curry seasoning (~420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Nan Gyi Thoke to small portions and request reduced fish sauce, as a standard serving may contribute approximately 64% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~34 per serving) from thick rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate source, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Nan Gyi Thoke in controlled portions (approximately 150–200 g) and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycemic response given the moderate glycemic load of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g) from chicken skin and cooking oil, combined with high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request preparation with less oil and reduced fish sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium intake, as regular consumption of full-sized servings may contribute to elevated blood pressure and LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nan Gyi Thoke (နန်းကြီးသုပ်) is a traditional Burmese noodle salad dish originating from Myanmar. The name translates literally as 'thick noodle salad', referring to the distinctive thick, round, smooth rice noodles (nan gyi) that form the base of the dish. The noodles are boiled until tender and then hand-tossed with a rich, aromatic chicken curry sauce, chickpea flour (to add body and a slightly nutty flavour), fish sauce, fried onions, garlic, turmeric, and dried chilli. The dish is typically garnished with sliced hard-boiled egg, fresh coriander, crispy fried onions, and a squeeze of lime juice. The texture is soft and silky from the noodles, with a savoury, mildly spiced, and slightly tangy flavour profile. Nan Gyi Thoke is a popular street food and home-cooked meal in Myanmar and is increasingly found in Burmese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from chicken and egg, and carbohydrates primarily from rice noodles. The dish is high in sodium due to fish sauce, which is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load food per serving. It is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Nan Gyi Thoke was found in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for Nan Gyi Thoke as a composite dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry becomes available. 2. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as 'Italian' cuisine with tags 'Pizza, Coffee, Toast' — these are artefacts of the field survey restaurant (The Light Cafe) rather than the dish itself. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Burmese'. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in rice noodle and chicken curry components per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero. 7. GI set to 53 based on analogous estimate from cooked rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 8. GL per serving = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75 g. 9. The dish is served at The Light Cafe (High Street Plaza, Singapore) as part of a Burmese menu section despite the restaurant's primary Italian/café branding.
